system: Archetypal large public research university bureaucracy in an OECD country

patterns.name: Central governing board and executive as singleton governance layer
patterns.domain_mapping: A single governing board and a single chief executive (president/rector) exercise ultimate formal authority over the university, establishing a globally accessible decision point for strategy, budget, and risk.
risks: Single point of failure for strategic, financial, and reputational decisions
Bottlenecks when all major approvals are funneled through one office
Potential capture of the institution by a small clique of actors
opportunities: Ability to shift global constraints or priorities relatively quickly if the node is responsive
recommended_refactorings: Introduce polycentric governance by delegating defined decision rights to faculties and cross-cutting councils
Replace some direct approval responsibilities with policy-level constraints and post-hoc audits

patterns.name: Student information system as facade over fragmented back-office processes
patterns.domain_mapping: A unified student portal exposes a simplified interface for enrolment, fees, results, and services, while internally orchestrating calls to numerous legacy finance, HR, curriculum, and records subsystems owned by different units.
risks: Interface stability masks growing internal technical and organizational debt
Incidents at any downstream system manifest as generic front-end failures with little transparency
Incentive to patch behind the facade rather than modernize core processes
opportunities: Single integration surface to progressively replace legacy components (Strangler Fig)
Ability to insulate students from some internal instability while refactoring proceeds
recommended_refactorings: Instrument the facade with fine-grained telemetry tied to back-office owners
Define explicit contracts and SLAs between the portal and each subsystem

patterns.name: Hierarchical approvals as chain of responsibility
patterns.domain_mapping: Requests such as hiring, promotions, curriculum changes, and research approvals are passed up a fixed line-management chain (supervisor → head of school → dean → provost), each with an opportunity to approve, reject, or escalate.
risks: High latency when every step is sequential and synchronous
Diffusion of responsibility: each actor can claim the decision was made elsewhere
Opaque failure modes when requests stall without explicit rejection
opportunities: Clear escalation paths for exceptional cases
Local filtering of low-quality or non-compliant proposals before they consume central bandwidth
recommended_refactorings: Split chains by decision type with explicit termination points and timeouts
Introduce parallel review (e.g., compliance checks in parallel with academic review) where dependencies allow

patterns.name: Faculty and student governance bodies as observer networks
patterns.domain_mapping: Faculty senates, school committees, and student associations subscribe to policy and governance events (e.g., proposed regulations, fee changes) and react via advice, resolutions, or protests.
risks: Notification without real influence produces performative consultation
Information overload if too many low-salience events are broadcast
Reactive rather than anticipatory governance when observers only see late-stage proposals
opportunities: Distributed sensing of emerging problems across the academic core
Potential for early detection of unintended consequences of policy changes
recommended_refactorings: Define event schemas with priority levels and required response types
Give observer bodies veto or delay rights over specific classes of change to make observation consequential

patterns.name: Enrolment, fees, and subscriptions embedding roach motel and forced continuity
patterns.domain_mapping: It is much easier for students to enrol, sign up for ancillary services, and consent to data use than to withdraw, suspend, cancel subscriptions, or revoke consent; exit paths are fragmented and procedurally expensive.
risks: Erosion of student trust and reputational damage
Regulatory exposure where consumer or privacy law demands symmetry of entry and exit
Hidden accumulation of inactive or distressed students inflating headline metrics
opportunities: Redesign flows to treat withdrawal and consent revocation as first-class supported actions
Use exit data as a leading indicator of structural problems (curriculum, support, finance)
recommended_refactorings: Apply an explicit symmetry constraint: any action that can be taken in N clicks must be reversible in comparable effort
Move from auto-renewing defaults to explicit, recurrent opt-in for non-essential services

patterns.name: Enterprise systems and central analytics as god object
patterns.domain_mapping: A small number of enterprise systems and central data warehouses accumulate many loosely related responsibilities – identity, finance, curriculum, research reporting, risk, and analytics – becoming indispensable and overloaded.
risks: Any outage or change propagates widely across operations and reporting
Local units become dependent on central teams for even minor changes
Architecture ossification as new capabilities are continually bolted onto the same platforms
opportunities: Use the central view to identify cross-cutting redundancies and candidate domains for modularisation
Progressively carve out coherent subdomains into dedicated platforms with clear APIs
recommended_refactorings: Apply domain-driven design to split the god object into bounded contexts (e.g., identity, curriculum, research admin)
Impose a moratorium on adding new unrelated responsibilities to the existing core without a sunset plan

patterns.name: Compliance layering as decorator proliferation
patterns.domain_mapping: New compliance, quality assurance, and risk requirements are addressed by adding review layers, checklists, and sign-offs around existing processes without simplifying the core workflows.
risks: Process accretion increases cycle time and cognitive load
Responsibilities become obscured as multiple committees and officers partially cover the same risk
Staff and academics experience change fatigue and disengage from the intent of controls
opportunities: Separate truly orthogonal controls (e.g., ethics, safety) from those that can be integrated into core processes
Retire obsolete or redundant layers when new systemic controls come online
recommended_refactorings: Introduce periodic control refactoring cycles that must remove or consolidate at least as many checks as they add
Model compliance chains explicitly and eliminate duplicate decorators that do not add distinct information

patterns.name: Shadow systems and workarounds as spaghetti code
patterns.software_analog: Spaghetti Code / Big Ball of Mud
patterns.domain_mapping: Faculties and departments implement local spreadsheets, scripts, and ad-hoc tools that interconnect informally with central systems, creating opaque and fragile dependencies.
risks: High operational risk concentrated in a few key individuals
Inconsistent data definitions and reporting across units
Difficulty isolating failures or performing global changes
opportunities: Use discovery of shadow systems as a map of unmet needs and candidate domains for platform investment
Elevate robust local solutions into supported shared services where appropriate
recommended_refactorings: Run regular dependency-mapping exercises to surface shadow integrations
Create low-friction, supported pathways to turn local scripts into governed microservices or workflows
